title: Sony Deletes 551 Movies PlayStation Owners Paid For
titleZh: 索尼删除 PlayStation 用户付费购买的 551 部电影
author: bilsbie
relevanceReason: 命中 1 个 AI/科技关键词。
summaryRaw: Ownership is slowly moving onto servers a few companies control, and the storefront hasn’t admitted it yet. Sony plans to wipe 551 movies and TV shows from the PlayStation Store libraries of customers who paid full price for them. The deletion is coming on September 1 and so far the company has said nothing about giving anyone their money back. The titles all come from StudioCanal, the distributor behind Terminator 2, Total Recall, Rambo: First Blood, The Deer Hunter, Bridget Jones’s Diary, From Dusk Till Dawn, and Cliffhanger. Anyone who hit “buy” on one of them will open their library that morning and find a hole where it used to be. PlayStation’s notice states it without apology: “You will no longer be able to access your previously purchased content from Studio Canal, and it will be removed from your video library.” The justification Sony offers runs to six words, “due to our content licensing agreements.” A licensing deal between Sony and StudioCanal expired or shifted, and the people who paid are the ones losing their films over it. None of them signed that contract and none gets a vote in it. X user somatyk surfaced the news on June 25, posting the notification they’d received. The message signed off with, “Click here for a full list of affected titles that will no longer be supported. Thank you.” Sony has since reproduced the same warning, and the full roster of 551 titles, on the PlayStation website.

Nobody rented these movies. The store put a “buy” button next to them, charged the purchase price, and dropped them into a library it called yours. Sony can empty that library the moment a contract somewhere upstream changes, and the terms of service you scrolled past on first boot already say you agreed to this. If the movie case feels abstract, the games industry just made the same point with its biggest release in over a decade. GTA 6 arrives November 19, and the boxed copy you can buy at Walmart or GameStop contains no disc. Take-Two confirmed it in a press release: “The physical version of Grand Theft Auto VI, containing a download code inside the box, will be available starting November 12, 2026 to support pre-loading.” You pay $80 for a cardboard sleeve wrapped around a download code that locks the game to your account. You cannot lend it, resell it, or install it offline. Do you want an actual disc that lives on your shelf and answers to no server? There isn’t one, and if a real physical edition ever ships, you’ll buy the game a second time to get it. Asked earlier whether Rockstar might hold physical copies back to stop leaks, Take-Two CEO Strauss Zelnick had said, “That’s not the plan.” The plan, it turns out, was to keep the box and throw away the disc. Killing the disc also ends the secondhand market and lending, and it hands the publisher control over access that a physical object never gave them. Ownership is being pulled back to the center, into accounts and servers a handful of companies control, and the words on the storefront haven’t caught up. The word “buy” might still sit on the button, but the definition of what that means keeps getting thinner.

comments.author: nullsmack
comments.textRawHtml:
We need some kind of modern equivalent to the old proposed Digital Media Consumer's Rights Act but which protects people's rights to digital media they buy. These should never be sold and then taken away with no compensation like this. We need a law that forces companies to treat digital files the same as a physical purchase. They can't take it away and have to allow people to resell and loan out as well. And in cases of online games where you can buy something, and then later they can ban you which deprives you of being able to use what you bought, that should come with requirements that the company must provide full compensation of the purchase price. It should also ban EULA's and TOS from defining these things as only licenses even though they are structured as a purchase in a store.

I know it'll never happen with the people we have in government these days, and the anti-consumer organizations, like the ESA, that are out there now claiming things like running private servers for Minecraft is illegal and piracy. (Yes, they really said that. Despite the fact that Minecraft has always provided the server and allowed this for 15+ years)
comments.textZhHtml:
我们需要某种与旧提议的《数字媒体消费者权利法案》相当的现代法案，但它可以保护人们对其购买的数字媒体的权利。这些东西绝对不应该被出售然后在没有任何补偿的情况下被拿走。我们需要一项法律，迫使公司像对待实体购买一样对待数字文件。他们不能把它拿走，还必须允许人们转售和借出。如果是在线游戏，你可以购买一些东西，然后他们可以禁止你，从而剥夺你使用你购买的东西的权利，这应该要求公司必须全额赔偿购买价格。它还应该禁止 EULA 和 TOS 将这些东西定义为仅许可证，即使它们的结构是在商店中购买的。

我知道这种事永远不会发生在我们现在的政府人员和反消费者组织中，比如欧洲航天局，他们现在声称为《我的世界》运行私人服务器是非法的和盗版的。 （是的，他们确实这么说。尽管 Minecraft 一直提供服务器并允许这样做超过 15 年）

children.author: hodgehog11
children.textRawHtml:
Not sure if you know this, but this is literally the Stop Killing Games movement. Despite the recent apparent setback as reported in the news, the organisers are in talks with EU MPs that are writing sweeping legislation to address this sort of thing across all digital mediums.

You only need legislation like this to hold in one major market to make a big difference.
children.textZhHtml:
不确定你是否知道这一点，但这实际上是“停止杀戮游戏”运动。尽管新闻报道中最近出现了明显的挫折，组织者仍在与欧盟议员进行谈判，他们正在起草全面的立法，以解决所有数字媒体上的此类问题。

你只需要这样的立法来维持一个主要市场就能产生巨大的变化。

children.author: Buttons840
children.textRawHtml:
The GP comment mentioned Minecraft servers. The full story is that California politicians were discussing Stop Killing Games and and that gamers were willing and capable of running their own servers, and ESA argued against that by saying private servers are illegal, basically.

Politicians are taking about it. Anyone who purchases media cares about it. Support for copyright reform is only going to grow, so hopefully we'll see some.
children.textZhHtml:
GP 评论提到了 Minecraft 服务器。完整的故事是，加州政客正在讨论停止杀戮游戏，并且游戏玩家愿意并且有能力运行自己的服务器，而欧洲航天局对此表示反对，称私人服务器基本上是非法的。

政治家们正在考虑这个问题。任何购买媒体的人都会关心它。对版权改革的支持只会越来越多，所以希望我们能看到一些。

children.author: throwaway287391
children.textRawHtml:
> You only need legislation like this to hold in one major market to make a big difference.

Could you explain this? I understand that for things like emissions regulations on cars, where it’s very expensive to invest R&D and production capacity into different SKUs for different markets. I can’t see why it would help in other markets in this case though (beyond potentially inspiring similar regulations elsewhere).
children.textZhHtml:
> 你只需要这样的立法来维持一个主要市场就能产生巨大的变化。

你能解释一下吗？我了解，对于汽车排放法规之类的事情，将研发和生产能力投资到不同市场的不同SKU上是非常昂贵的。但我不明白为什么在这种情况下它会对其他市场有所帮助（除了可能激发其他地方的类似法规之外）。

children.author: roganartu
children.textRawHtml:
The most relevant example of this for the games industry is probably Steam’s refund policy. They allow you to refund a game if you bought it less than 2 weeks ago and have less than 2 hours playtime recorded.

This is their policy for all users, but was a direct result of an Australian consumer watchdog lawsuit [0].

[0] https://www.accc.gov.au/media-release/valve-to-pay-3-million...
children.textZhHtml:
对于游戏行业来说，最相关的例子可能是 Steam 的退款政策。如果您在不到 2 周前购买游戏并且记录的游戏时间少于 2 小时，他们允许您退款。

这是他们针对所有用户的政策，但却是澳大利亚消费者监管机构诉讼的直接结果 [0]。

[0] https://www.accc.gov.au/media-release/valve-to-pay-3-million...

children.author: simjnd
children.textRawHtml:
Counter-example to this would be 3rd-party app stores on iOS, which are only enabled in the EU, Japan and more recently in Brazil (maybe I'm missing some).

Legislation in one market didn't impact the state of things in the others, and some countries get screwed over.
children.textZhHtml:
与之相反的例子是 iOS 上的第 3 方应用商店，这些商店仅在欧盟、日本以及最近在巴西启用（也许我遗漏了一些）。

一个市场的立法并没有影响其他市场的状况，有些国家就被搞砸了。

children.author: throwaway85825
children.textRawHtml: EU parliament unlike nearly every other parliament can't propose legislation, only amend it. That is because it's a fake parliament and the EU is a fake democracy to give the people the illusion that the EU cares about them.
children.textZhHtml: 与几乎所有其他议会不同，欧盟议会不能提出立法，只能对其进行修改。那是因为它是一个假议会，欧盟是一个假民主国家，让人们产生欧盟关心他们的错觉。

children.author: holgerschurig
children.textRawHtml:
You are both rigth and wrong.

First, the EU parliament isn't a "fake" parliament. You just claimed this, without any reasoning, so I'm free to just claim the opposite. The members of this parliament are freely elected and they can decide out on their own whim, without consulting the executive. So really they are one of the 3 legs, the legislative.

However, the "EU is a fake democracy" is maybe correct.

The thing is that one should never confuse the EU with some sovereign country, like the Unites States of America. Or with a federal state like the Federal Republic of Germany. The origins of the EU aren't based in some idea of nationality. They are based in trade: how to make trade easier, how to create a common market. It's much more similar to the "Hanse" than than to a modern national state.

And as such, comparing the EU with a national state is futile.

It's actually astounding that this trade club got so many democratic institutions, like the EU parliament or the EU court of justice. A lot of other trade agreements only have fully undemocratic arbitration tribunals.

Also, "the EU cares about" is silly. The EU is a body of many members. The member states think not in unions (just think of "Visegrad members"). This list here isn't even complete:

- Council of the European Union headed by Antonia Costa - EU Commission headed by Ursula von der Leyen - EU Parliament headed by Roberta Metsola - European Central Bank headed by Christine Lagarde - European Court of Auditors headed by Tony Murphy - Court of Justice headed by ... no idea

So one part can "care" about a thing, while another part does not. One must have lived under a rock to not know how e.g. the Commission often wanted some Orwell-1984 laws, again and again, and the EU parliament or the EU court of justice dismissed them. So no, the EU isn't a uniform body. And any claim in that direction is therefore inherently false.
children.textZhHtml:
你既对又错。

首先，欧盟议会不是“假”议会。你只是这么说，没有任何理由，所以我可以自由地提出相反的观点。这个议会的成员是自由选举产生的，他们可以根据自己的想法做出决定，而无需咨询行政部门。所以他们实际上是立法的三条腿之一。

然而，“欧盟是一个假民主”也许是正确的。

问题是，人们永远不应该将欧盟与某个主权国家（例如美利坚合众国）混淆。或者像德意志联邦共和国这样的联邦国家。欧盟的起源并非基于某种国籍观念。它们以贸易为基础：如何使贸易变得更容易，如何创建共同市场。它更像是“汉萨同盟”，而不是现代民族国家。

因此，将欧盟与民族国家进行比较是徒劳的。

实际上令人震惊的是，这个贸易俱乐部拥有如此多的民主机构，例如欧盟议会或欧盟法院。许多其他贸易协定只有完全不民主的仲裁法庭。

此外，“欧盟关心”是愚蠢的。欧盟是一个由许多成员国组成的机构。成员国不考虑工会（只需考虑“维谢格拉德成员”）。这里的列表还不完整：

- 安东尼娅·科斯塔领导的欧盟理事会 - 乌苏拉·冯德莱恩 (Ursula von der Leyen) 领导的欧盟委员会 - 欧盟议会由罗伯塔·梅索拉 (Roberta Metsola) 领导 - 克里斯蒂娜·拉加德领导的欧洲央行 - 托尼·墨菲领导的欧洲审计院 - 法院的领导者是……不知道

因此，一部分可以“关心”一件事，而另一部分则不然。一个人一定是生活在一块岩石下才不知道如何，例如委员会经常一次又一次地想要一些奥威尔 1984 年的法律，但欧盟议会或欧盟法院却驳回了它们。所以不，欧盟不是一个统一的机构。因此，任何朝这个方向的主张本质上都是错误的。

children.author: munksbeer
children.textRawHtml:
Misleading misinformation. The EU laws come from direction set by the Council (elected representatives from the national parliaments). Then their chosen civil service (the Commission) formulate and propose those laws based on the direction set by the Council. Then the EU elected MPs vote on the laws.

That is not less democratic than how many countries operate.
children.textZhHtml:
误导性的错误信息。欧盟法律来自理事会（各国议会选出的代表）制定的方向。然后，他们选择的公务员（委员会）根据理事会制定的方向制定并提出这些法律。然后欧盟当选议员对法律进行投票。

这并不比许多国家的民主程度低。

children.author: M95D
children.textRawHtml:
Considering the kind of people that get voted into Parliament, I consider that a very good thing.

I only consider true democracies those countries where citizens can force new legislation by national referendum, bypassing parliament. There are very very few countries that allow it, and even fewer where the requirements are low enough to actually allow it to happen.

Anyway, back to the subject. If Stop Killing Games will ever be law, it will be in EU. We already have very nice things like GDPR, USB charging, and replaceable batteries (soon).
children.textZhHtml:
考虑到那些被选入议会的人，我认为这是一件非常好的事情。

我只考虑真正的民主国家，即公民可以绕过议会，通过全民公投强制实施新立法的国家。允许这种情况发生的国家非常少，而要求足够低而实际上允许这种情况发生的国家就更少了。

无论如何，回到主题。如果“停止杀戮游戏”成为法律，那一定是在欧盟。我们已经有了一些非常好的东西，例如 GDPR、USB 充电和可更换电池（很快）。

children.author: throwaway85825
children.textRawHtml: The replaceable batteries is only a mandate that it retain 80% capacity after n charge cycles, which phones already meet. Toothless and useless regulation, the EU strikes again.
children.textZhHtml: 可更换电池只是要求在 n 个充电周期后保留 80% 的容量，而手机已经满足了这一要求。无效且无用的监管，欧盟再次出击。

children.author: hodgehog11
children.textRawHtml: I think we know from using Athens of Ancient Greece as an example that true democracies of this kind are not a good idea at scale. Enough of the general public can be so easily swayed on a clearly catastrophic idea.
children.textZhHtml: 我认为，以古希腊雅典为例，我们知道这种真正的民主在规模上并不是一个好主意。足够多的公众很容易被一个明显灾难性的想法所左右。

children.author: M95D
children.textRawHtml: Responsibility comes with practice. Switzerland is doing okay.
children.textZhHtml: 责任来自于实践。瑞士表现还不错。

children.author: pipes
children.textRawHtml:
I think this is going to have unintended consequences if it goes through: funding game development is a very very high risk investment. Adding this type of regulation adds further cost and complication, which makes it an even less attractive investment. Which will result in less monetary and creative risk taken.

Even just open sourcing part of project is expensive. Legal and technical.
children.textZhHtml:
我认为如果这件事发生的话将会产生意想不到的后果：为游戏开发提供资金是一项风险非常高的投资。添加此类监管会进一步增加成本和复杂性，从而使其成为一项更具吸引力的投资。这将减少货币和创意风险。

即使只是项目的开源部分也是昂贵的。法律和技术。

children.author: ryandrake
children.textRawHtml:
This argument is always pretty weak. All regulation adds cost and complication for companies. If that alone was a good reason to not add regulation, then taken to the logical conclusion, there should be no regulation on companies.

Regulation needs to be seen as a trade-off, for example: We get better behaving companies, and the cost is less risk taken. Then the question becomes "is the benefit worth the cost?"
children.textZhHtml:
这个论点总是很薄弱。所有监管都会增加公司的成本和复杂性。如果仅凭这一点就可以成为不增加监管的充分理由，那么从逻辑上看，就不应该对公司进行监管。

监管需要被视为一种权衡，例如：我们得到了表现更好的公司，而代价是承担的风险更少。那么问题就变成了“收益是否值得付出代价？”

children.author: Ferret7446
children.textRawHtml:
The argument should be stronger since the vast majority of regulation is as you say, the cost is not worth the benefit.

All regulation by default is anticompetitive and also comes with the risk of intended consequences, and thus must prove that they provide sufficient benefit. They are "guilty until proven innocent".

e.g., rather than trying to "just do something" about games, maybe we should revisit copyright law as a whole, instead of slapping a bandaid on a festering wound caused by the first bandaid
children.textZhHtml:
这个论点应该更有力，因为绝大多数监管正如你所说，成本不值得收益。

默认情况下的所有监管都是反竞争的，并且还伴随着预期后果的风险，因此必须证明它们提供了足够的好处。 他们“在被证明无罪之前是有罪的”。

e.g., rather than trying to "just do something" about games, maybe we should revisit copyright law as a whole, instead of slapping a bandaid on a festering wound caused by the first bandaid

children.author: benj111
children.textRawHtml:
How is all regulation anti competitive?

Is it anti competitive that your product does what you say it does and is safe? Isn't that just levelling the playing field by allowing consumers to make rational choices?

What about regulation designed to make sure there is competition? Is that anticompetitive???
children.textZhHtml:
所有监管如何都是反竞争的？

你的产品按照你所说的那样做并且是安全的，这是否构成反竞争？这不就是公平竞争，让消费者做出理性选择吗？

旨在确保竞争的监管又如何呢？这算反竞争吗？？？

children.author: Schiendelman
children.textRawHtml: I thought this was more "let people run the code themselves if they can figure it out" not "open sourcing". There's no appetite to force a company that reuses and improves their game engine on every new title to open source it, even an older version.
children.textZhHtml: 我认为这更多的是“让人们自己运行代码，如果他们能弄清楚的话”而不是“开源”。没有兴趣强迫一家在每个新游戏中重用和改进其游戏引擎的公司将其开源，即使是旧版本。

children.author: Volundr
children.textRawHtml:
That alone would be an improvement from the status quo, but the SKG movement does go one stop further in wanting publishers to be required to leave the game in a working (local) state of they design it to be dependent on central services they then shut down.

This could be as easy as releasing the tools they used for development when developing the game.
children.textZhHtml:
That alone would be an improvement from the status quo, but the SKG movement does go one stop further in wanting publishers to be required to leave the game in a working (local) state of they design it to be dependent on central services they then shut down.

这可能就像在开发游戏时发布他们用于开发的工具一样简单。

children.author: Schiendelman
children.textRawHtml:
That's really tough. Marathon, by Bungie, is a good example of why this is tough - it's basically all the Destiny engine, network, and tooling code. When they shut down Destiny, it's not like they have a snapshot of their tooling. They've been continuously updating that tooling for ten years, and now they're using that investment for a new project.

Most game studios are similar, in reusing and improving a whole development architecture and systems across many titles. I would not agree with making them release that, even an older version. That's a big competitive moat for some studios.

I think if a community group wants to PAY to operate a server, that's quite reasonable. And then you still end up with a fight about how much to charge. But I don't think handing over server code is the right move.
children.textZhHtml:
这真的很难。 Bungie 的 Marathon 是一个很好的例子，说明了为什么这很困难 - 它基本上是 Destiny 引擎、网络和工具代码的全部。当他们关闭《命运》时，他们并没有工具的快照。十年来他们一直在不断更新该工具，现在他们正在将这笔投资用于一个新项目。

大多数游戏工作室在重用和改进许多游戏的整个开发架构和系统方面都是相似的。我不同意让他们发布这个版本，即使是旧版本。对于一些工作室来说，这是一条巨大的竞争护城河。

我认为如果一个社区团体想要付费来运营服务器，这是相当合理的。然后你们仍然会因为收费多少而争论不休。但我不认为移交服务器代码是正确的举动。

children.author: hodgehog11
children.textRawHtml:
I don't think that's what working state means here. It's not a full snapshot or even necessarily multiplayer support. It means minimum functional, which to me is just barely enough to see the assets used in the game. Not necessarily networking, matchmaking, online services, or the relevant tooling. Developers have already proposed making helpers that would introduce an effective switch to do this.

Small like 30 people? I don't think so. Genuinely small studios are able to preserve their games for the long-term, so there is no excuse.

AAA was always an untenable monster that brought obscene risk; this was clear even in the mid-2010s and the industry is rapidly moving away from that model for good reason. The user-base clearly does not look kindly on AAA anymore either. That's why they are not profitable anymore. The SKG requirements would make very little impact overall compared to this.

AAA始终是一个难以忍受的怪物，带来了令人厌恶的风险；即使在 2010 年代中期，这一点也很明显，并且该行业正在迅速摆脱这种模式，这是有充分理由的。用户群显然也不再对 AAA 抱有好感了。这就是他们不再盈利的原因。与此相比，SKG 要求总体影响很小。

children.author: hodgehog11
children.textRawHtml:
Absolutely! Happy to learn from an industry veteran; hard to argue with that pedigree (part of why I like HN). I figured you might have been, but wanted to push back a little because I think it is important.

Here is my impression. 2001 feels like it was still within a golden era of game development; "AAA" games of that time would have been made by smaller studios still, and budgets could be very large, but not catastrophically so. The industry was still expanding. Post-GFC, once graphics scaled, demands seemed to scale, and costs blew out. Games had to reduce risk as a consequence, become more consolidated, more live-service. But the model was never sustainable at that scale. The tech improved so that costs for basic games went down, but big-budget AAA live service costs went to the moon. Volatility skyrocketed, leading to rapid hiring-firing phases. Now it is at its most extreme and the AAA side of the industry is in crisis. Demands for long-term support could be the straw that breaks the camel's back, but it always seems like that back was going to break eventually anyway. What I hope is happening is that talent is falling into the hands of smaller publishers, but that might not be true. What I do think is that the nature of development may need to change so that studios are able to facilitate these requirements while remaining profitable. Some have shown it can be done, anyway.

That's my impression from a semi-outsider perspective. Happy to be corrected though.
children.textZhHtml:
绝对地！乐于向行业资深人士学习；很难与这个血统争论（这也是我喜欢 HN 的原因之一）。我想你可能是这样，但想稍微推迟一下，因为我认为这很重要。

这是我的印象。 2001 年感觉仍处于游戏开发的黄金时代；当时的“AAA”游戏仍然是由较小的工作室制作的，预算可能非常大，但也不会是灾难性的。该行业仍在不断扩张。全球金融危机后，一旦图形规模扩大，需求似乎也会扩大，成本也会大幅上升。因此，游戏必须降低风险，变得更加整合、更加实时化。但这种模式在如此规模上永远无法持续。技术的改进使得基本游戏的成本下降，但大预算的 AAA 实时服务成本却飙升。波动性急剧上升，导致快速的招聘-解雇阶段。现在已经到了最极端的时候，AAA级的行业已经陷入了危机。对长期支持的需求可能是压垮骆驼的最后一根稻草，但无论如何，这根背似乎最终都会折断。我希望正在发生的事情是人才落入较小的出版商手中，但这可能不是事实。我确实认为，开发的性质可能需要改变，以便工作室能够在保持盈利的同时满足这些要求。无论如何，有些人已经证明这是可以做到的。

这是我从一个半局外人的角度来看的印象。不过很高兴被纠正。

children.author: acdha

I’d go with a simple proposal: if you use the word “buy” the seller is required to refund buyers if the seller’s action removes access (unlike, say, losing access to a CD or DVD after the disc is scratched).

The most likely outcome is that they’d change their storefronts to use the word “rent” but that’s a good outcome (it encourages buyers to accurately understand what they’re paying for) and it would allow other options like releasing an old game without DRM prior to killing servers.
children.textZhHtml:
我会提出一个简单的建议：如果您使用“购买”一词，如果卖方的行为删除了访问权限（与光盘被划伤后失去对 CD 或 DVD 的访问权限不同），则卖方需要向买家退款。

最有可能的结果是他们会更改店面以使用“租用”一词，但这是一个很好的结果（它鼓励买家准确了解他们所支付的费用），并且允许其他选择，例如在关闭服务器之前发布没有 DRM 的旧游戏。

Fortunately, we don't need a policy solution or to convince already-bought-and-paid-for-lawmakers to side with justice over profits.

Solutions for liberating media of all kinds, from P2P file sharing to DRM-stripping, have roundly and soundly outpaced all this other corporate knowledge control nonsense at every turn since the invention of the printing press.

All we need to do is make the simple choice to stop recognizing ownership of ideas (and thus, bytes) as a conceptually coherent phenomenon, and carry on.

I'm a full-time professional bluegrass musician, and like most of my peers, I release all of my material DRM-free. I invite you to get my new record from IPFS or Bit Torrent, and to pin/seed/encourage your friends to steal it also:

children.author: garciansmith
children.textRawHtml:
Yep. I had tons of Sony games across the first three Playstation consoles. I was a grad student with a PS3 at the time and I actually used Yellow Dog Linux on it as a computer to write papers when my laptop broke. Then the update came and I chose to ignore it, but that meant I couldn't play online games. Soon new games required a firmware update (still remember putting in the Dark Souls disc and being stunned I wasn't allowed to play it!).

And with games it's just getting worse (Sony announced they won't make discs starting 2028; the Switch 2 takes carts but very, very few games release on a cart). If you care about control over the games you purchased, if you care about going back and playing older games, then the only choice is to use platforms that are DRM free. (Or, well, non-legal means.)

对于游戏来说，情况变得更糟（索尼宣布从 2028 年开始不再制作光盘；Switch 2 需要推车，但很少有游戏是在推车上发布的）。如果您关心对购买的游戏的控制，如果您关心返回并玩旧游戏，那么唯一的选择就是使用无 DRM 的平台。 （或者，嗯，非法手段。）

children.author: Shalomboy
children.textRawHtml:
Valve is in a funny position now. They lived long enough to see every one of Sony and XBOX's moats dry up by being pro-consumer where possible, but with Steam as the leader of a fungible game distribution market it may no longer make good business sense to continue to act so benevolently.

We've reached a sort of gaming singularity where nearly every video game can be run on any hardware you choose or be streamed over the network to a thin client. PlayStation and XBOX consoles are basically dedicated gaming PCs that can only run Sony or Microsoft's version of Steam. DirectX is losing ground too thanks to Proton and Vulkan, so Microsoft won't have the last laugh there either. If Valve controls the store you purchase games from, the software which runs the games, and the operating system running the software, they are an ODM contract away from becoming Sony's PlayStation division, and look where they are now.
children.textZhHtml:
Valve 现在处于一个有趣的位置。 They lived long enough to see every one of Sony and XBOX's moats dry up by being pro-consumer where possible, but with Steam as the leader of a fungible game distribution market it may no longer make good business sense to continue to act so benevolently.

We've reached a sort of gaming singularity where nearly every video game can be run on any hardware you choose or be streamed over the network to a thin client. PlayStation 和 XBOX 游戏机基本上是专用游戏 PC，只能运行索尼或微软版本的 Steam。由于 Proton 和 Vulkan，DirectX 也在节节败退，因此微软也不会笑到最后。 If Valve controls the store you purchase games from, the software which runs the games, and the operating system running the software, they are an ODM contract away from becoming Sony's PlayStation division, and look where they are now.

Also every big publisher wanting to pull their own games to their own storefront and only being forced to crawl back because gamers refuse to leave Steam

And even if they somehow arrived in a market position where being less benevolent would make more money: Valve isn't publicly traded, nobody is forcing them to make the most profitable move. As long as Gabe and the other owners prefer being benevolent they can continue doing it

(not that they are all around benevolent. "consistent" and "usually choosing the side of the customer over the side of the publisher" is maybe the better framing)

children.author: bsimpson
children.textRawHtml:
> DirectX is losing ground too thanks to Proton and Vulkan

[citation needed]

My understanding is that Proton is effectively a distribution of WINE + DXVK, which does for DirectX what WINE does for Win32. Valve is even on record saying "make one good copy of your game and trust our compatibility tools to port to other platforms" which is effectively saying "use DirectX if you want - Proton will run it just as well."
children.textZhHtml:
> 由于 Proton 和 Vulkan，DirectX 也在节节败退

[需要引用]

我的理解是，Proton 实际上是 WINE + DXVK 的发行版，它对 DirectX 的作用相当于 WINE 对 Win32 的作用。 Valve 甚至有记录称“为您的游戏制作一份良好的副本，并相信我们的兼容性工具可以移植到其他平台”，这实际上是在说“如果您愿意，可以使用 DirectX - Proton 也可以运行它”。

children.textZhHtml:
事实上，律师们提出了“想象财产”的概念，称之为“知识产权”，并为自己围绕这个骗局建立了自己的生意，而其他人却认真对待它，这是人类糟糕透顶的一些最令人信服的证据。

在数字时代，复制本身的成本在功能上为零，我们应该一次又一次地向人们支付曾经创造过的东西，就像人类利用自己的优势一样。

我通过劳动、新奇事物和生产东西来赚钱，但此后重复付款对任何理性的外国人来说都像是一个骗局。

“版权”是人类最贪婪和自私的表现。

children.author: dylan604
children.textRawHtml: I like how succinct this is.
children.textZhHtml: 我喜欢这句话的简洁性。

children.author: noahbp
children.textRawHtml:
Plenty of people purchase digital movie rentals from Apple, Youtube, etcetera because they know they will watch it once, and the lower price in exchange for a temporary license is acceptable to them. I don't think banning this is pro-consumer.

It should, however, be illegal to tell your customers that they are purchasing/buying media without explicit "Rent" language (which implies a non-expiring license) when you do not yourself have the right to grant non-expiring licenses.
children.textZhHtml:
很多人从 Apple、Youtube 等购买数字电影租赁服务，因为他们知道自己会观看一次，并且以较低的价格换取临时许可证是他们可以接受的。我不认为禁止这一点是有利于消费者的。

但是，当您自己无权授予未过期许可证时，在没有明确“租赁”语言（这意味着未过期许可证）的情况下告诉您的客户他们正在购买/购买媒体应该是违法的。

children.author: etempleton
children.textRawHtml: They often have two tiers, a rental tier and a purchase tier. If you purchase the assumption is it will be available forever.
children.textZhHtml: 它们通常有两个层次：租赁层和购买层。如果您购买，则假设它将永远可用。

children.author: dylan604
children.textRawHtml: Seems like a bad assumption at this point even if it goes against expectations. We've seen on multiple occasions now from different companies where a digital purchase wasn't forever. This is no way an endorsement of the behavior, but if that's your assumption then the quip "you know what happens when you assume" wins again.
children.textZhHtml: 在这一点上似乎是一个糟糕的假设，即使它违背了预期。我们已经在不同的公司多次看到数字购买并不是永远的。这绝不是对这种行为的认可，但如果这是你的假设，那么“你知道当你假设时会发生什么”这句俏皮话再次获胜。

children.author: crote
children.textRawHtml:
So the default assumption should be that big companies are actively trying to defraud you?

The difference between "rent" and "purchase" has always been very clear: with the former you have to give it back, with the latter you own it forever. It is only very recently that this kind of steal-back has even become possible at all, as it can only be done with digital content delivery to an entirely-closed platform.

"You should assume that words have the opposite meaning and that big companies can steal from you with impunity" is a world I don't think I want to live in.
children.textZhHtml:
那么默认的假设应该是大公司正在积极地试图欺骗你？

“租”和“买”的区别一直很明显：前者你必须归还，后者你拥有它 永远。这只是 非常 最近这种偷回甚至成为可能 根本不，因为这只能通过将数字内容交付到完全封闭的平台来完成。

“你应该假设词语具有相反的含义，大公司可以从你那里窃取而不受惩罚”，这是一个我认为我不想生活在其中的世界。

children.author: dylan604
children.textRawHtml:
Whether you want to or not, that's pretty much where we live now. A default position of "every company is trying to defraud me" is not a bad position to take. As you find out a company is less of a criminal, then you can relax your position, but if you go in relaxed and need to tighten up, you've probably already been screwed out of something.

The "rent" vs "purchase" has never been different than it is now. Yes, purchase is a very deceiving word by design. The thing bringing it to the forefront is that they never were able to rip that physical product sold to you in the way they can with the digital product. That seems to have made people pay attention to the legalese that has always been there.
children.textZhHtml:
不管你愿意与否，这就是我们现在住的地方。 “每家公司都试图欺骗我”的默认立场并不是一个坏的立场。当你发现一家公司不再是罪犯时，你就可以放松你的立场，但如果你放松地进入并需要收紧，你可能已经被骗了。

“租”与“买”从来没有像现在这样不同过。是的，购买这个词从设计上来说就是一个极具欺骗性的词。最重要的是，他们永远无法像数字产品那样撕毁卖给你的实体产品。这似乎让人们开始关注一直存在的法律术语。

children.author: Klonoar
children.textRawHtml: It’s not a bad assumption to the average person because words have meanings, and in this context they go back decades.
children.textZhHtml: 对于普通人来说，这是一个不错的假设，因为单词是有意义的，在这种情况下，它们可以追溯到几十年前。

children.author: babypuncher
children.textRawHtml:
> Plenty of people purchase digital movie rentals from Apple, Youtube, etcetera because they know they will watch it once, and the lower price in exchange for a temporary license is acceptable to them. I don't think banning this is pro-consumer.

Many of these services offer cheaper rental options. When you go for the more expensive "buy" option, the assumption that you are actually buying it to keep should hold true.
children.textZhHtml:
> 很多人从 Apple、Youtube 等购买数字电影租赁，因为他们知道自己会观看一次，并且以较低的价格换取临时许可证是他们可以接受的。我不认为禁止这一点是有利于消费者的。

其中许多服务提供更便宜的租赁选择。当您选择更昂贵的“购买”选项时，您实际上购买它是为了保留的假设应该成立。

children.author: cryptonym
children.textRawHtml: Agree... if they want to sell it, parent company must agree on forever licenses for each user. Regardless of reselling license getting cancelled.
children.textZhHtml: 同意...如果他们想出售它，母公司必须同意每个用户的永久许可证。无论转售许可证是否被取消。

children.author: 404mm
children.textRawHtml:
I bet there’s a class action coming.

And Sony made it easy for them too by using this verbiage: “previously purchased content”
children.textZhHtml:
我打赌即将发生集体诉讼。

索尼也通过使用这样的措辞让他们变得容易：“以前购买的内容”

children.author: bilekas
children.textRawHtml: Because they were purchases, not rentals. Under no circumstances would a customer reasonably assume that their purchase would be revoked for reasons completely outside of their control.
children.textZhHtml: 因为它们是购买，而不是租赁。在任何情况下，客户都不会合理地认为他们的购买会因为完全超出其控制范围的原因而被撤销。

children.author: throwaway87543
children.textRawHtml: Studio Canal directly got paid for each individual purchase.  It isn't all on Sony, Studio Canal sold a product then took it away.
children.textZhHtml: Studio Canal 直接从每次购买中获得报酬。  这不全是索尼的事，Studio Canal 卖掉了产品然后把它拿走了。

children.author: crote
children.textRawHtml:
> Studio Canal sold a product then took it away

If that's the case, why isn't Sony suing Studio Canal? The proper license for Sony to get is one which doesn't allow Studio Canal to take it away after sale in any way. So did Studio Canal somehow hack into Sony HQ to violate that license, or did Sony simply screw over its customers by not getting the proper license in the first place?
children.textZhHtml:
> Studio Canal 出售了产品然后将其拿走

如果是这样的话，索尼为什么不起诉 Studio Canal？索尼获得的正确许可是不允许 Studio Canal 在售后将其拿走的许可 以任何方式。那么，Studio Canal 是否以某种方式侵入了索尼总部并违反了该许可证，或者索尼只是因为一开始就没有获得适当的许可证而欺骗了其客户？

children.author: ndsipa_pomu
children.textRawHtml:
Not wishing to let them off the hook, but consumers did not enter into a contract with Studio Canal, so it's somewhat irrelevant to the actual issue. Sony willingly entered into a contract with Studio Canal and then "sold" items to consumers that they could reasonably foresee would be time limited without making that clear to the purchasers.

I'd like to see Sony executives put in prison for fraud for this.
children.textZhHtml:
不想让他们摆脱困境，但消费者没有与 Studio Canal 签订合同，所以这与实际问题有些无关。索尼自愿与 Studio Canal 签订合同，然后向消费者“出售”他们可以合理预见到有时间限制的物品，但没有向购买者明确说明。

我希望看到索尼高管因此因欺诈而入狱。

children.author: babypuncher
children.textRawHtml:
They're both to blame. Studio Canal insisted on a licensing agreement that works this way, and Sony agreed to it to sell their content.

For Sony, the correct move here would have been to not list Studio Canal titles in the first place, and put out a very public statement saying that they aren't being listed until Studio Canal agrees to make purchased licenses perpetual as they should be.
children.textZhHtml:
他们都有责任。 Studio Canal 坚持以这种方式签订许可协议，索尼也同意它出售他们的内容。

对于索尼来说，正确的做法是首先不列出 Studio Canal 的作品，并发表一份非常公开的声明，表示在 Studio Canal 同意将购买的许可按应有的方式永久化之前，它们不会被列出。

children.author: Terr_
children.textRawHtml:
> Both to blame

IANAL but I think the US law approach is to rely on chaining, so the #1 blame is on Sony until Sony proves it isn't.

1. Consumers who were damaged sue Sony for damages.

2. If Sony loses, Sony sues Studio Canal for damages.

3. If Studio Canal loses... ?
children.textZhHtml:
> 双方都有责任

IANAL 但我认为美国法律的做法是依赖链接，因此第一大责任归咎于索尼，直到索尼证明事实并非如此。

1. 受到损害的消费者起诉索尼要求赔偿。

2.如果索尼败诉，索尼将起诉Studio Canal，要求赔偿损失。

3. 如果 Studio Canal 输了……？

children.author: matwood
children.textRawHtml: Correct. In theory, Sony should have warranted that they have the rights to sell the thing the way they sold it. If they didn't have the rights to sell a movie perpetually, then that's on them.
children.textZhHtml: 正确的。从理论上讲，索尼应该保证他们有权按照他们销售的方式销售该产品。如果他们没有永久出售电影的权利，那就是他们的责任。

children.author: crote
children.textRawHtml: They don't need the rights to sell a movie perpetually, just the rights to sell perpetual movies.
children.textZhHtml: 他们不需要永久出售电影的权利，只需要永久出售电影的权利。

comments.author: NoSalt
comments.textRawHtml: Ever since Apple, Microsoft, Google, etc. started offering "free" online storage for photos, ever since streaming started to be popular, I have ALWAYS extolled the virtues of µSD card slots in phones and owning your own media (i.e., purchasing CDs and DVDs). Many people would give me a hard time about this, calling me a Luddite, but I will never lose access to my photos, music, or movies ... unless it is the end of the world as we know it, which I happily have on R.E.M.'s Eponymous album.
comments.textZhHtml: 自从苹果、微软、谷歌等开始提供“免费”在线照片存储，自从流媒体开始流行以来，我一直称赞手机中的 µSD 卡插槽和拥有自己的媒体（即购买 CD 和 DVD）的优点。许多人会因此对我感到难受，称我为勒德分子，但我永远不会失去对我的照片、音乐或电影的访问权……除非这是我们所知的世界末日，我很高兴在 R.E.M. 的同名专辑中拥有它。

children.author: NoPicklez
children.textRawHtml:
From my experience with SD cards in mobile phone when I worked in a Telco, most customers didn't actually know how to use them. Because in many cases plugging in the SD card didn't magically increase the base storage of the device, they actually needed to know how to store things on it.

But that's a separate issue altogether
children.textZhHtml:
从我在电信公司工作时使用手机SD卡的经验来看，大多数客户实际上并不知道如何使用它们。因为在许多情况下，插入 SD 卡并不会神奇地增加设备的基本存储空间，他们实际上需要知道如何在上面存储内容。

但这完全是一个单独的问题

children.author: mort96
children.textRawHtml: I just wish it was possible to buy decent ųSD cards. Even really expensive ones sold in reputable stores die all the time. Sure they work just fine if your goal is to write photos into it, never delete anything and get a new one once it fills up, but anything more write heavy typically kills my SD cards within months. SSDs last decades.
children.textZhHtml: 我只是希望能够买到像样的 ųSD 卡。即使是在信誉良好的商店出售的非常昂贵的产品也总是会死掉。当然，如果您的目标是向其中写入照片，永远不要删除任何内容并在存储满后获取新的，那么它们就可以正常工作，但是任何写入量更大的内容通常会在几个月内耗尽我的 SD 卡。 SSD 可以使用数十年。

children.author: Gigachad
children.textRawHtml: An SD card should only ever be treated as a local cache for data you can replace or will soon move to more permanent storage. Even if you had the best card in the world you could still lose or have your phone stolen.
children.textZhHtml: SD 卡只能被视为本地缓存，用于存储您可以替换的数据或很快就会转移到更永久的存储。即使您拥有世界上最好的卡，您仍然可能丢失或手机被盗。

children.textZhHtml: 我希望图书馆有磁带写入器和读取器。磁带存储对于数据备份和归档来说非常经济高效。

children.author: tristor
children.textRawHtml:
> Does anyone actually do this?

Yep. I've been using an iPhone since 2012, so about 14 years. I do a local backup to my Macbook and then perform upgrades via USB, I don't do over the air upgrades. I also don't sync my photo albums into iCloud. I do have iCloud backups enabled also. It's not even a concern about privacy, per se, it's a question of whether or not I actually control my data if its not in my possession. More copies is good for resiliency.
children.textZhHtml:
> 真的有人这么做吗？

是的。 我从 2012 年开始使用 iPhone，大约有 14 年了。 我对 Macbook 进行本地备份，然后通过 USB 执行升级，我不进行无线升级。 我也不会将我的相册同步到 iCloud。 我也启用了 iCloud 备份。 本质上，这甚至不是对隐私的担忧，而是如果数据不属于我的话，我是否真正控制它的问题。 更多副本有利于恢复能力。

children.author: Gigachad

children.textZhHtml: iPhone 从未有过 microSD 插槽，早在 icloud 出现之前很多年。

children.author: gilfaethwy
children.textRawHtml: Nothing really wrong with being a Luddite. The Luddites weren't anti-technology or anti-progress. Rather, they opposed deployment of technology in the workplace which they believed would reduce their pay, reduce the quality of their output, and make it easier to exploit vulnerable people such as children. They were, basically, doing the same thing as software engineers who argue that AI mandates are making their lives worse and making the quality of their employers' software worse.
children.textZhHtml: 成为勒德分子并没有什么错。勒德分子并不反技术或反进步。相反，他们反对在工作场所部署技术，他们认为这会减少他们的工资，降低他们的产出质量，并更容易剥削儿童等弱势群体。基本上，他们所做的事情与软件工程师相同，他们认为人工智能的强制要求使他们的生活变得更糟，并使雇主的软件质量更差。

comments.author: Danii27
comments.textRawHtml:
The honest product description would be: "You are purchasing a revocable license to stream this content at our discretion, for an unspecified period, subject to change without notice."

Nobody would buy that. So they say "buy" instead, and courts have largely let them get away with it. Until legislation actually forces the word "buy" to mean ownership, this will keep happening.
comments.textZhHtml:
诚实的产品描述是：“您正在购买可撤销的许可证，以便我们自行决定在未指定的期限内流式传输此内容，如有更改，恕不另行通知。”

没有人会买那个。所以他们改为说“购买”，而法院基本上让他们侥幸逃脱惩罚。在立法真正强制“购买”一词意味着所有权之前，这种情况将会持续发生。

children.author: Buttons840
children.textRawHtml:
I disagree. People would still buy that.

There's a dangerous path where the companies are required to describe these transactions as "rentals", but that wont actually solve anything. If we require clearer advertising, we're going to end up with a world where everything is very clearly a rental, and there simply is no option to purchase. People will still buy the $40 "rentals" because it's their favorite movie and they want to watch it multiple times, and it's Friday night and they want to watch it right now.

I think people understand the situation when they "purchase" digital media. They know it might not last forever. They do it anyway. They don't like it though. They would prefer genuine ownership, but it's not an option.

We either need to outlaw these long term rentals, or break up monopolies until companies that are actually offering genuine purchases arise. Or we could do both.

We need to regulate more than just the wording on the "purchase" page. This isn't just a problem of wording.
children.textZhHtml:
我不同意。人们仍然会购买。

这是一条危险的道路，公司被要求将这些交易描述为“租赁”，但这实际上并不能解决任何问题。如果我们需要更清晰的广告，那么我们最终将面临一个一切都非常明显是租赁的世界，而且根本没有购买的选择。人们仍然会购买 40 美元的“租赁”，因为这是他们最喜欢的电影，他们想多次观看，而且现在是周五晚上，他们想立即观看。

我认为人们在“购买”数字媒体时就了解这种情况。他们知道这可能不会永远持续下去。无论如何他们都会这么做。但他们不喜欢它。他们更喜欢真正的所有权，但这不是一个选择。

我们要么需要取缔这些长期租赁，要么打破垄断，直到真正提供真正购买服务的公司出现。或者我们可以两者都做。

我们需要规范的不仅仅是“购买”页面上的措辞。这不仅仅是措辞的问题。

children.textZhHtml: 我认为这作为一种价格锚定工具会很有帮助：人们认为租金价值较低，并接受更大的限制，因为他们支付的费用较少。我认为你不会看到那么多人支付 40 美元的限时租金，而且这种下行压力是健康的。

children.author: temporallobe
children.textRawHtml:
> I think people understand the situation when they "purchase" digital media. They know it might not last forever.

I don’t think this is true at all because 1) truly nothing is forever, i.e., even physical media degrades over time or gets lost, stolen, etc., and 2) the customer is absolutely led to believe that “purchase” means ownership similar to having a physical copy, as long as that service provider exists. Otherwise it’s clearly a rental.
children.textZhHtml:
> 我认为人们在“购买”数字媒体时就了解这种情况。他们知道这可能不会永远持续下去。

我认为这根本不是真的，因为 1) 确实没有什么是永远的，即，即使物理介质也会随着时间的推移而退化或丢失、被盗等，2) 客户绝对会相信“购买”意味着与拥有物理副本类似的所有权，只要该服务提供商存在。否则，这显然是出租。

children.author: crote
children.textRawHtml:
I think it is quite tricky to argue that StudioCanal did anything wrong.

Sony isn't a naive child, they've got whole armies of lawyers analyzing the deals they sign. If Sony knowingly signs a horrible deal (such as a Netflix-like "you can show this bunch of content to users and let them download it for X years") which due to their desire to sell movies leads to them inevitably getting screwed over on their (now-mandatory) contract renewal, that's on them.

Either Sony should've insisted on a contract which included a right to sell perpetual sublicenses, or they shouldn't have "sold" those inherently-temporary movies to end users. It really is that simple.
children.textZhHtml:
我认为争论 StudioCanal 所做的事情是相当棘手的 任何事物 错误的。

索尼不是一个天真的孩子，他们有大批律师分析他们签署的交易。如果索尼故意签署 可怕 交易（例如类似 Netflix 的“你可以向用户展示这堆内容并让他们下载 X 年”），这是由于他们希望 卖 电影导致他们不可避免地在续约（现在是强制性的）合同上陷入困境，这是他们的责任。

索尼要么应该坚持签订包含出售永久再许可权利的合同，要么他们不应该将这些本质上是临时的电影“出售”给最终用户。确实就是这么简单。

I wonder if legal concepts such as “constructive larceny” or “theft by conversion” could be applied here, or if this would be considered destruction of property?

Movies Anywhere works by aggregating the active digital access licenses you have participating partner services. The problem here is Sony won't be able to give any active digital access licenses for these movies to users after September 1st, let alone one which allows them to transfer that through Movies Anywhere.

It's typical "you own nothing" logic to the point the companies selling you that also don't even own it.

children.author: zamadatix
children.textRawHtml:
It's more "streaming/delivery operators which also have some of their own licensed content":

- Disney -> Disney+/Hulu

- Universal/NBC -> Peacock

- Warner Bros. -> Max

- Lionsgate -> Lionsgate+

- Sony Pictures -> The Sony services relevant to the article (note: the Sony services sell more than just Sony Pictures content)

They never needed to, but it actually makes them more money because a revenue share model through Movies Anywhere makes sense. StudioCanal does not sell streaming/delivery services directly to consumers, a revenue share model between streaming providers would not make them more money, and Sony would have no influence on StudioCanal doing so anyways.
children.textZhHtml:
它更像是“流媒体/交付运营商也拥有一些自己的许可内容”：

- 华纳兄弟 -> 麦克斯

- 狮门影业 -> 狮门影业+

- 索尼影业 -> 与本文相关的索尼服务（注意：索尼服务销售的不仅仅是索尼影业内容）

他们从来不需要这样做，但这实际上让他们赚了更多钱，因为通过 Movies Anywhere 的收入分成模式是有意义的。 StudioCanal 不直接向消费​​者出售流媒体/传输服务，流媒体提供商之间的收入共享模式不会让他们赚更多钱，而且索尼无论如何也不会影响 StudioCanal 这样做。

children.author: nekusar
children.textRawHtml:
gitflic.ru is the Russian Github that permits speech that the DMCA doesn't.

And yes, bypassing DRM is banned speech in the USA, punishable by criminal law. And there's no actual requirement of a company to claim DRM and defeat methods. The law is set up so they can claim basically anything, and its 100% backed by criminal law.

1FA is kinda a joke, cause saying inane shit like "Hitler was a good guy" is perfectly fine, but "09 F9 11 02 9D 74 E3 5B D8 41 56 C5 63 56 88 C0" (HD-DVD private key) is criminally banned speech.
children.textZhHtml:
gitflic.ru 是俄罗斯的 Github，允许发表 DMCA 不允许的言论。

是的，在美国，绕过 DRM 的言论是被禁止的，会受到刑法的惩罚。并且公司并没有实际要求声明 DRM 和破解方法。法律的制定是为了让他们基本上可以提出任何要求，而且它 100% 受到刑法的支持。

1FA 有点像个笑话，因为说“希特勒是个好人”之类的空话完全没问题，但“09 F9 11 02 9D 74 E3 5B D8 41 56 C5 63 56 88 C0”（HD-DVD 私钥）是刑事禁止言论。

但我一刻也不相信“购买”数字产品，而且我很快就被证明是对的。 第一次数字购买被撤的故事距今大概已近 20 年前了。

当无法获得无 DRM 的数字同等产品时，我仍然购买 CD、书籍和游戏光盘。

children.author: acdha
children.textRawHtml:
> But I not for a single second trusted "buying" digital goods, and I was quickly proved right. The first digital purchases getting yanked story must have been close to 20 years ago at this point.

More like 30 years but your terminology is slightly off: I have music I bought in the 90s which still works fine because they sold it as MP3s without DRM and I was able to make my own backups. There’s nothing wrong with digital goods as a concept but we should be consistent in how we talk about it: if it involves an outside service, it’s a rental no matter how the seller describes it. If you can make your own copy and survive a corporate bankruptcy or merger, then it’s a real purchase.
children.textZhHtml:
> 但我一刻也不相信“购买”数字产品，而且我很快就被证明是对的。第一次数字购买被撤的故事距今大概已近 20 年前了。

更像是 30 年，但你的术语有点偏差：我有一些我在 90 年代购买的音乐，它们仍然可以正常工作，因为他们将其作为没有 DRM 的 MP3 出售，而且我能够制作自己的备份。数字商品作为一个概念并没有什么问题，但我们在谈论它的方式上应该保持一致：如果它涉及外部服务，无论卖家如何描述它都是租赁。如果您可以制作自己的副本并在公司破产或合并中幸存下来，那么这就是真正的购买。
